notes.note: Lyso of Patrae was Cicero's hospes (guest-friend) in the Greek sense: a hereditary bond of mutual hospitality, distinct from mere friendship. Cicero had stayed with Lyso during his time in Patrae in the aftermath of Pharsalus (48 BC). The follow-up letter 13.24 confirms that an earlier recommendation (now lost) had already been sent.

notes.anchor: he was in the habit of speaking at Rome against your standing
notes.note: An accusation that Lyso had slandered Servius's dignitas (standing, reputation) at Rome. Dignitas was a Roman aristocrat's most prized social-political asset; even a rumour of disparagement in the capital could damage a proconsul's position and post-provincial career.

notes.anchor: thanks to your easy temper and humanity
notes.note: Facilitas (easy temper) and humanitas (cultivated benevolence) are paired as the marks of a just and educated provincial governor; Cicero frequently deploys this flattery in recommendation letters to characterise the recipient as already inclined toward clemency.

notes.anchor: he has accomplished everything by my one letter
notes.note: Cicero refers to the earlier letter of recommendation to Servius on Lyso's behalf (probably Fam. 13.19 or a now-lost predecessor). The remark illustrates the weight that a high-profile recommendation letter from a consular could carry in a provincial governor's court.
